Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard source array with a tagged release tarball from the project's official GitHub repository (`https://github.com/rustyhorde/moshpit/archive/v$pkgver.tar.gz`) and a local service file. The `sha256sums` are provided and match both the archive and the local `mps.service` file (with a note explaining the duplicated hash for CI purposes). 

There is no code in the global scope or within `pkgver()` that would execute commands during sourcing. The `pkgver` is hard-coded as `0.9.2`, so no dynamic version calculation (which could involve command substitution) occurs. As a result, sourcing this PKGBUILD during `makepkg --printsrcinfo` or `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will not trigger any unexpected network requests, file modifications, or code execution.

All operations that involve potentially sensitive commands (like `cargo fetch` or `cargo build`) are confined to the `prepare()` and `build()` functions, which are not executed under `--noprepare` and `--nobuild`. Therefore, the risk during source fetching and PKGBUILD parsing is negligible.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious code in sourcing or source fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No malicious code in sourcing or source fetch[/]

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains no top-level command substitutions, `pkgver()` function, or other code that executes during sourcing. The `source` array downloads a tarball from the project's own GitHub repository (`https://github.com/rustyhorde/moshpit/archive/v$pkgver.tar.gz`) and a local sidecar file. No obfuscated, encoded, or unusual operations are present. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only fetch and extract the standard upstream source; no malicious code is executed during this step. The identical SHA256 checksums for two different files are a packaging oversight (likely a copy-paste error) but not a security threat for this narrow safety gate.
</details>
<summary>
Standard PKGBUILD; no dangerous execution during source fetch or parse.
</summary>
</security_assessment>
